The retired West Indies cricketer aims to optimize her local team using data-driven strategies. Her model for the average batting score is defined by the quadratic equation \( S = 50 + 5n - 0.2n^2 \). To find the number of practice sessions \( n \) that maximizes this score, we must locate the vertex of the parabola. Since the coefficient of \( n^2 \) is negative, the parabola opens downward, indicating a maximum point exists. The vertex occurs at \( n = -b / (2a) \), where \( a = -0.2 \) and \( b = 5 \). Calculating this gives \( n = -5 / (2 \times -0.2) \), which simplifies to \( n = 12.5 \). Since practice sessions are typically whole numbers, we check integers 12 and 13. At \( n = 12 \), \( S = 50 + 60 - 28.8 = 81.2 \). At \( n = 13 \), \( S = 50 + 65 - 33.8 = 81.2 \). Both values yield the same maximum score of 81.2 runs. Thus, the optimal number of sessions is 12 or 13, resulting in a maximum average batting score of 81.2.

For the second part, we calculate the change in win probability when sessions increase from 5 to 10. First, we find the score at \( n = 5 \): \( S_5 = 50 + 25 - 5 = 70 \). Next, we find the score at \( n = 10 \): \( S_{10} = 50 + 50 - 20 = 80 \). Using the logistic formula \( P = 1 / (1 + e^{-(0.1S - 3)}) \), we compute \( P_5 \). The exponent becomes \( -(0.1 \times 70 - 3) = -4 \), so \( P_5 = 1 / (1 + e^{-4}) \approx 0.9820 \). For \( P_{10} \), the exponent is \( -(0.1 \times 80 - 3) = -5
